coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots and fullerenol as new carbon sources for single–layer and bi–layer graphene synthesis by rapid thermal annealing method
coredata.dc:creator: Prekodravac, Jovana R.
Marković, Zoran M.
Jovanović, Svetlana P.
Holclajtner-Antunović, Ivanka D.
Kepić, Dejan P.
Budimir, Milica D.
Todorović-Marković, Biljana M.
coredata.dc:description: Graphene as a new material is in the spotlight due to its extraordinary properties and wide range of potential applications. Chemical vapour deposition, as a method for graphene synthesis from gaseous hydrocarbon sources has great promises for large–scale graphene synthesis. However, for such grow high temperatures of 800–1000°C are typically required. Here we demonstrate synthesis of single–layer and bi–layer graphene thin films of approximately 10μm in size by rapid thermal annealing process at low annealing temperature such as 600°C. Synthesis was performed on copper foil from graphene quantum dots and fullerenol as solid carbon sources at different concentrations. Raman spectroscopy study has shown that the number of grown graphene layers depends on the carbon source concentration. The quality of formed graphene layers withal depends on the carbon source and its concentration, whereby graphene from graphene quantum dots had better quality.
coredata.dcterms:subject: A. Nanostructures
A. Thin films
C. Raman spectroscopy
C. Transmission electron microscopy
D. Defects

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Step one: preparation of Cu foil
Step three: electroless deposition of metal thin film with carbon source
Step four: graphene synthesis and transfer

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the SASPRO Programme project 1237/02/02-b. The research leading to these results has received funding from the People Programme (Marie Curie Actions) European Union's Seventh Framework Programme under REA grant agreement No. 609427. Research further has been co-funded by the Slovak Academy of Sciences. Research was also supported by the Ministry of Education, Science and Technological Development of the Republic of Serbia (2011-2016) project no. 172003, Serbian-French bilateral project (2014-2015) project no. 451-03-3455/2013-09/08, bilateral project Serbia-Slovakia SK-SRB-2013-0044 (451-03-545/2015-09/07), and VEGA(2/0093/16).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Raman spectra stacked from bottom to top with increase in carbon source concentration from 0.25mgml−1, 0.50mgml−1, 0.75mgml−1 to 1mgml−1: a) GQD and b) fullerenol.
The 2D band analysis from samples with GQD as carbon source in different concentrations: a) change in 2D band intensity, b–e) Lorentz curve fit of the 2D band.
The 2D band analysis from samples with fullerenol as carbon source: a) change in 2D band intensity, b–e) Lorentz curve fit of the 2D band.
SEM images of graphene thin films synthesized from: (a) GQD 0.25mgml−1, (b) GQD 0.75mgml−1, (c) fullerenol 0.25mgml−1 and (d) fullerenol 0.75mgml−1.
TEM images of formed graphene thin films synthesized from: (a) GQD 0.25mgml−1, (b) GQD 0.75mgml−1, (c) fullerenol 0.25mgml−1 and (d) fullerenol 0.75mgml−1.
Data collected from Raman spectra presented in Fig. 1a, b.
EDS analysis of formed graphene layers.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots and fullerenol as new carbon sources for single–layer and bi–layer graphene synthesis by rapid thermal annealing method

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Vinča Institute of Nuclear Sciences, University of Belgrade, Mike Alasa 12-14, 11001 Belgrade, Serbia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Polymer Institute, Slovak Academy of Sciences, Dubravska Cesta 9 Bratislava, Slovakia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Physical Chemistry, University of Belgrade, Studentski trg 12-16, 11158 Belgrade, Serbia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene as a new material is in the spotlight due to its extraordinary properties and wide range of potential applications. Chemical vapour deposition, as a method for graphene synthesis from gaseous hydrocarbon sources has great promises for large–scale graphene synthesis. However, for such grow high temperatures of 800–1000°C are typically required. Here we demonstrate synthesis of single–layer and bi–layer graphene thin films of approximately 10μm in size by rapid thermal annealing process at low annealing temperature such as 600°C. Synthesis was performed on copper foil from graphene quantum dots and fullerenol as solid carbon sources at different concentrations. Raman spectroscopy study has shown that the number of grown graphene layers depends on the carbon source concentration. The quality of formed graphene layers withal depends on the carbon source and its concentration, whereby graphene from graphene quantum dots had better quality.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene is a two-dimensional material with honeycomb structure that has become one of the most widely used atomic crystal structure materials since its first experimental confirmation in 2004 [1]. Owing to its optical, electrical, mechanical and electrochemical properties [2,3], it has attracted attention of many researches. These properties propose interesting applications of graphene ranging from electronics to biomedicine, to energy storage and conversion [4–6].

Chemical vapour deposition (CVD) and Rapid thermal annealing (RTA) are the most used methods for graphene synthesis nowadays. Graphene growth by these methods was previously demonstrated on a variety of metal substrates [7–10]. However, nickel (Ni) [11,12] and copper (Cu) [13] are unique between potential metal substrates because Cu has low carbon solubility where its surface acts as a self-limiting catalyst for graphene growth, while in Ni carbon has very high solubility [14,15]. By introducing Cu and Ni as substrates for graphene growth, the size, thickness and quality of produced graphene approach industrially useful specifications [16–18]. Up to now, many solid precursors were used for graphene synthesis, such as camphor, poly (methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) or hydrocarbons [19–23], but the temperature range at which synthesis are performed is rather high.

Here we report for the first time growth of single–layer (SLG) and bi–layer graphene (BLG) thin films from graphene quantum dots (GQD) and fullerenol as solid carbon precursors by RTA method. Carbon sources in different concentrations were deposited on a metal catalyst substrate (Cu) during electroless Ni plating. Samples were than annealed at temperature as low as 600°C for 30min under vacuum. By electrochemical delamination process, grown graphene thin films were transferred onto silicon (Si) substrate. We found SLG thin films of good quality at lower carbon source concentrations while the formation of BLG was observed at higher concentrations. The number of graphene layers (NGL) and their quality were determined by Raman spectroscopy, while the average size, morphology and quantitative chemical analysis of grown graphene thin films were investigated with Scanning electron microscopy, Energy dispersive spectroscopy and Transmission electron microscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Step one: preparation of Cu foil
ce:section.ce:para:
Cu foil (99.99% pure) was cut into small pieces of 1cm2 in size. To remove organic impurities from Cu surface, the Cu foil was dipped into acetone for 5min and then into methanol, for the same time, to remove acetone residuals. After that, Cu foil was washed with DI water and dried. Next, we performed sensitization and activation of Cu surface. The sensitization was achieved by dipping Cu substrates into solution of SnCl2
× 2H2O (5 mgml−1) for 5min. After washing substrates with DI water we proceeded the activation of Cu surface by dipping it into the acid water solution of PdCl2 (0.02 mgml−1) for 5min [24]. The washed and dried Cu foil was prepared for the electroless deposition of metal film.

Synthesis of fullerenol with 44 hydroxyl groups on average was performed in one-step from pristine C60, where synthesis employed a hydrogen peroxide aqueous solution/toluene bi-layer system with an appropriate phase-transfer catalyst [25]. For GQD synthesis we performed electrochemical procedure using graphite rods as both anode and cathode and sodium hydrogen/ethanol mixture as electrolyte [26].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Step three: electroless deposition of metal thin film with carbon source
ce:section.ce:para:
The electroless plating is defined as autocatalytic deposition of metals from an aqueous solution of its ions by interaction with a chemical reducing agent, where reducing agent provides the electrons necessary for converting metal ions (Mn+) to the metal form (M0). Complexion agent and bath stabilizer on the other hand accelerate the deposition process and prevent precipitation of the metal salt [24]. Electroless deposition was performed by preparing 40ml of Ni–bath solution containing 10ml of each solution: source of metal ions NiCl2
× 6H2O (6.6 mgml−1), reducing agent NaH2PO2 x H2O (20mgml−1), complexion agent Na3C6H5O7
× 2H2O (45 mgml−1) and NH4Cl (30 mgml−1) as bath stabilizer. In 40ml of prepared solution, we added either fullerenol or GQD as a carbon source in concentrations of 0.25, 0.50, 0.75 and 1mgml−1. The presence of hydrophilic groups such as hydroxyl or carboxyl group on the surface of fullerol and GQD allows good dispersion of carbon source in the water plating solution. The pH value of Ni–bath solution with carbon source was adjusted to 9 using ammonium hydroxide (NH4OH). Even though a wide range of baths with pH values from 4 to 11 is possible, at acid pH values the deposited metal film will have an increased phosphorous content that drops significantly in basic baths. The bath temperature during electroless deposition was maintained in a range of 70–80°C. Metal deposition rate increases exponentially with temperature from 60 to 90°C, where below 60°C deposition does not occur while above 90°C the metal bath decompose [24]. The prepared Cu substrates with Pd particles on its surface were immersed into Ni–carbon bath where we could observe the deposition of Ni–carbon composite film. Therefore, carbon sources were easily incorporated into metal film during the metal deposition process. After 5min of deposition, the Cu substrates with Ni–carbon films on its surface (Cu/Ni/fullerenol or Cu/Ni/GQD) were washed with DI water and dried.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Step four: graphene synthesis and transfer
ce:section.ce:para: Prepared samples (Cu/Ni/fullerenol and Cu/Ni/GQD) were annealed at 600°C for 30min using an RTA system under vacuum (10−3 Torr; TorVac United Kingdom). Annealing was followed by rapid cooling to form graphene [27]. For graphene transfer we deposited thin film of PMMA dissolved in toluene by drop casting on the sample surface as a protection layer (Cu/graphene/PMMA). For better binding of PMMA to graphene, samples (Cu/graphene/PMMA) were heated at 150°C for 10min. The electrochemical delamination process was conducted in NaOH water solution (10 mgml−1), where Ni wire was used as anode and Cu/graphene/PMMA sample was used as cathode. Applied current and voltage were 20mA and 2V, respectively. After delamination, graphene/PMMA layers were transferred to Si substrate (Si/graphene/PMMA) and TEM copper grid for further characterization, while the PMMA was removed using hot acetone vapour.

With DXR Raman microscope (Thermo Scientific), 532nm laser as excitation source, we obtained Raman spectra of transferred graphene samples. The laser power was kept at 2mW to avoid heating of the sample, with a pixel–to–pixel spectral resolution of 1cm−1.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was carried out on a QUANTA FEI 200 FEG-ESEM at 10–30kV in high vacuum conditions to characterize the surface of graphene thin films. Energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) was used to perform quantitative chemical analysis of the graphene samples.
The morphology of the graphene samples was also studied by Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) employing a TEM JEOL Jem 1210 Electron microscope operating at 120kV.
For SEM and TEM analysis graphene samples were prepared by transferring graphene thin films onto commercially available carbon coated TEM copper grid 200mesh.

Raman spectroscopy is non-destructive method suitable for quick inspection of the NGL [28]. Typically, the Raman spectra of graphene consists of three predominant bands denoted as the D, G and 2D bands [29], present by peaks at approximately 1350cm−1, 1580cm−1 and 2690cm−1, respectively, using a 532nm laser excitation [30].

The Raman spectra present in Fig. 1 are ordered from bottom to top according to increasing concentration of GQD (Fig. 1a) and fullerenol (Fig. 1b) used as carbon sources. Raman spectra are representative spectra recorded from several different spots of the sample, covering the region of 1200–3400cm−1. For clarity, spectra are normalized in respect to the 2D band intensity to give them all approximately the same intensity.

Several observations can be made from Fig. 1. Ferrari et al. [31] showed that it is possible to learn about the NGL in graphene samples by investigating the shape, position and intensity of the second order 2D feature in graphene Raman spectra. The 2D band for SLG at room temperature exhibits a single sharp 2D band with large intensity relative to the G band (I2D/IG >2) [32]. With increase in NGL, 2D band is generally wider and more asymmetric while the 2D to G band intensity ratio decreases (I2D/IG <2). By collecting data from Raman spectra presented in Fig. 1a and b, we observed very high I2D to IG ratio for samples at concentrations of 0.25mgml−1 and 0.50mgml−1. In samples with GQD, the I2D is approximately 6 times more intense than the IG, while in samples with fullerenol this ratio is approximately 5, which undoubtedly demonstrate formation of SLG. The increase in carbon source concentration led to significant decrease in I2D/IG bellow 2 (Table 1 ), for both carbon sources.

The increase in NGL is usually followed with downshift of the G band position (ωG) [33]. In our samples we observed the downshift in the G band position of ∼7cm−1 with increase in carbon source concentration (GQD or fullerenol). However, since the G band position is strongly dependent on the doping level [34], the more reliable way to identify the NGL is based on analyses of the 2D band [31]. In Figs. 2 a 3 a , we present the change in the 2D band intensity with increase in carbon source concentration (Table 1), GQD and fullerenol respectively.

As it is known, the 2D band in Raman spectra obtained from SLG is formed by one Lorentzian peak, while in BLG the 2D band is composed of four peaks [35] which is demonstrated in Figs. 2 b–e and 3 b–e for samples with GQD and fullerenol, respectively.

We measured full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the 2D bands (Table 1) at lower carbon concentrations (0.25mgml−1 and 0.50mgml−1), and observed that 2D bands FWHM of ∼26cm−1 are characteristic for SLG. However, at higher carbon concentrations the measured FWHM (2D) were ∼35cm−1 and characteristic for BLG [19].

Along with the existence of G and 2D band, in graphene Raman spectra (Fig. 1) we also observed the presence of two more bands. These bands are assigned as the 2D′ band, sharp second–order feature insensitive to the NGL that appears at ∼3248cm−1, and the additional weak G* band at ∼2450cm−1 [36].

The D band is not visible in a perfect hexagonal carbon lattice, since it is associated with the breathing oscillation of full carbon rings that are “forbidden” in a perfect hexagonal carbon lattice. Therefore, the D band defined as the defect or disorder band whose intensity (ID) is directly proportional to the level of defects present in the sample. The presence of D band in graphene Raman spectra (Fig. 1a, b), can be attributed to the electrochemical delamination process of PMMA supported graphene thin films from Cu substrate. As it was demonstrated earlier by Yang et al. [37], the electrochemical delamination rate depends on the delamination voltage, where higher voltages achieve faster delamination but also can introduce defects into graphene films. The increase in the D band intensity, with increase in source concentration is clearly noticeable from Fig. 1a and b. The defect level, defined by D to G band intensity ratio (ID/IG) indicates that graphene samples from GQD and fullerenol at lower concentrations are of comparable quality (Table 1). However, at higher carbon source concentrations (0.75mgml−1 and 1mgml−1) this ratio increases significantly for almost 2 times in GQD graphene samples to almost 4 times in graphene samples from fullerenol, comparing to lower concentrations. Beside the presence of the D band, at high carbon concentrations the Raman intensity shows rise in two more, separate disorder bands assigned as D′ band (∼1620cm−1) and D+G band (∼2940cm−1) [30]. The emergence in the D′ band is mostly from randomly distributed impurities or surface changes, while the presence of wick D+G disorder band at ∼2800–2950cm−1 may originate from PMMA covered graphene, since there was some residual PMMA after acetone removal [38,39]. The intensity of D+G disorder band in Raman spectra depends on the laser power [40], which is the reason for such a small intensity in the spectra (Fig. 1a, b) at 2mW.

Observations associated with the defect level in graphene samples at higher carbon concentrations indicate lower quality in samples synthesized from fullerenol comparing to the graphene grown from GQD. This remark can be associated with GQD and fullerenol structure. Namely, fullerenol as a polyhydroxylated fullerene [41] derivate consists of pentagons and hexagons with a high number of hydroxyl functional groups attached to the fullerene cage. Therefore, its transformation to graphene honeycomb lattice is more difficult, and may cause larger number of defects comparing to the thin, flat GQD [26] with hydrophobic sp2 hybridized graphene plane in the core and smaller number of hydrophilic functional groups (hydroxyl, carbonyl and carboxyl) on the edges. Defects in graphene structure may be present in the form of vacancies formed during electrochemical delamination process, or topological defects during transformation of fullerenol cage to graphene honeycomb lattice [42].

The use of SEM facilitates imaging and EDS analysis of graphene layers with respect to the substrate, providing a more direct analysis of graphene. Thus, the number of formed graphene layers can be determined by SEM imaging based on the surface roughness contrast, edge contrast or thickness contrast [43].

Fig. 4 presents SEM images of graphene samples synthesized from GQD and fullerenol in concentrations of 0.25mgml−1 and 0.75mgml−1. Raman spectroscopy confirmed formation of good quality SLG, and BLG with high defect level for both carbon sources at aforementioned concentrations. Fig. 4a, c presents the formation of SLG thin films synthesized from GQD and fullerenol respectively, at concentration of 0.25mgml−1. Evidently, the formed graphene thin films contain defects in forms of wrinkles, ruptures, folds and voids formed during graphene transfer process [37]. The presence of residual PMMA is also marked in the figure.

In Fig. 4b, we present BLG thin films obtained from GQD in concentration of 0.75mgml−1. Here we noticed differences in the surface contrasts and a morphology evolution with increase in carbon source concentration. These contrasts could be related to the change in the thickness of formed graphene layers as it was already reported in the literature [44]. Therefore, aside from predominant SLG regions, some small BLG regions marked by white arrows were clearly visible. The formed fragments are rather shapeless since their edges are not very well structured. Fig. 4d show graphene thin film obtained from fullerenol in concentration of 0.75mgml−1. Here we present very large graphene film display in high brightness forming white line along graphene edge (marked by white arrow) with small ripples and BLG islands. There is also residual PMMA attached to the graphene film. The lateral size of formed graphene layers was more than 5μm2 for SLG and more than 10μm2 for BLG.

The EDS analysis of graphene thin films show that the most abundant element in all graphene samples is carbon. The oxygen is detected in low percent. The present oxygen originates from residual PMMA, chemical formula (C5O2H8)n. In Table 2 we give the carbon:oxygen (C:O) ratios measured in atomic percent (At%) for all graphene samples.

The optical contrast of graphene layers on different substrates is the most simple and effective method for the identification of the number of layers. Thus, TEM can not only observe the morphological feature of graphene thin films but also count the number of graphene layers accurately [45].

Fig. 5 a–d presents graphene thin films synthesized from GQD and fullerenol in concentrations of 0.25mgml−1 and 0.75mgml−1. Fig. 5a shows the formation of SLG thin film from GQD in concentration of 0.25mgml−1 with a clear edge, and presence of residual PMMA in shape of dark grains. At higher GQD concentration (Fig. 5b) we could clearly distinguish small BLG regions marked by black arrows.

In Fig. 5 c we present SLG thin film synthesized from fullerenol in concentration of 0.25mgml−1 with traces of dark, shapeless PMMA residues. At higher fullerenol concentration (Fig. 5d) the formation of different graphene layers can be noticed, as it was confirmed earlier in this work. The lateral sizes of formed graphene layers presented in Fig. 5 were more than 5μm2.

In summary, we were able for the first time to perform graphene synthesis from GQD and fullerenol as a carbon source by RTA method on Cu substrate. This study shows that both GQD and fullerenol can serve as a carbon source for synthesis of SLG and BLG at low annealing temperature such as 600°C. The number of formed graphene layers and their quality strongly depends on a carbon source concentration. By comparing graphene samples synthesized from GQD and fullerenol in different concentrations, we have noticed the growth of SLG thin films with good quality at lower concentrations (0.25mgml−1 and 0.50mgml−1). Increase in carbon source concentration led to increase in NGL and formation of BLG with higher amount of defects. However, graphene thin films at these concentrations (0.75mgml−1 and 1mgml−1) grown from GQD had better quality.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the SASPRO Programme project 1237/02/02-b. The research leading to these results has received funding from the People Programme (Marie Curie Actions) European Union’s Seventh Framework Programme under REA grant agreement No. 609427. Research further has been co-funded by the Slovak Academy of Sciences. Research was also supported by the Ministry of Education, Science and Technological Development of the Republic of Serbia (2011-2016) project no. 172003, Serbian-French bilateral project (2014-2015) project no. 451-03-3455/2013-09/08, bilateral project Serbia-Slovakia SK-SRB-2013-0044 (451-03-545/2015-09/07), and VEGA(2/0093/16).
